semantic_journey: Arabic hatha = to scatter/strew; al-hatha = scattered soil/dust/strewn earth-matter. Greek khthoni = to/on the earth/ground (from khthon = earth, soil). Both encode earth/soil/ground. Arabic focuses on the particulate earth-matter; Greek on the earth as surface. Shared earth-matter semantic field. Plausible one-step alignment: scattered earth-matter -> the earth/ground.
key_insight: Arabic scattered dust/soil and Greek earth/ground share the earth-matter semantic field - plausible connection.

semantic_journey: Arabic khasam = opponent in dispute, adversary in legal contest. Greek I will fight (physical combat future tense). Legal adversary and physical fighter overlap only at the most general level of conflict. A legal adversary (khasam) is not the same as a fighter. Different types of conflict separated by the legal-vs-physical distinction.
key_insight: Arabic legal adversary/dispute and Greek physical fighting are different types of conflict - not a real match.

semantic_journey: Arabic dirham = silver coin. Greek drachmai = drachma. The Semitic dirham is acknowledged to derive from Greek drachme (via Aramaic). Arabic borrowed FROM Greek. Direction is Greek-to-Arabic via Aramaic. Not an Arabic-origin cognate for Greek.
key_insight: Arabic dirham borrowed FROM Greek drachme via Aramaic - Greek-to-Arabic direction, not the reverse.

semantic_journey: Arabic karmel = Mount Carmel (a mountain in the Levant). Greek Karmalos = Mount Carmel. Both name the same mountain. The name Carmel is an ancient Semitic toponym (k-r-m-l = garden land in Hebrew). Proper name identity - a mountain toponym, not a lexical root cognate.
key_insight: Both name Mount Carmel - a Semitic mountain toponym preserved in Arabic and Greek, not a root cognate.

semantic_journey: Arabic al-furat = the Euphrates River. Greek Euphrates = the Euphrates River. Both name the same ancient river. The river name derives from Old Persian Ufratush or Elamite Pu-ra-tu, not from a Semitic root. Arabic and Greek both independently adapted the ancient river name from Persian/Elamite substrate. Shared proper toponym.
key_insight: Both name the Euphrates River - ancient toponym from Persian/Elamite substrate, preserved in Arabic and Greek independently.

semantic_journey: Arabic al-qarmad = fired bricks, baked ceramic (khazaf matbukh = cooked pottery), also plaster - materials made by heating clay. Greek keramidion = small ceramic tile (diminutive of keramion, from kerameus = potter). Both refer to fired clay/ceramic construction materials. The Semitic q-r-m-d for fired ceramic may parallel Greek keram-. The semantic match is strong. Classifying as plausible link due to uncertainty about borrowing direction.
key_insight: Arabic fired bricks/ceramic and Greek ceramic tile share fired-clay material culture - plausible link, borrowing direction uncertain.

semantic_journey: Arabic al-ladhan = labdanum (the resinous gum of Cistus plants, used in perfumery). Greek ledanon = labdanum. Both name the same aromatic resin from Cistus shrubs, a luxury trade good from the eastern Mediterranean. The Semitic term for labdanum (l-d-n) entered Greek as ledanon. Confirmed material culture transmission of a named aromatic substance.
key_insight: Semitic labdanum term (l-d-n) entered Greek as ledanon - confirmed aromatic trade commodity loanword.

semantic_journey: Arabic salla = to draw/unsheathe (a sword), a sharp pulling motion to extract a blade. Greek psallo = to pluck (a string), to twitch/pull with the fingers. Both encode a sharp drawing/pulling motion. Arabic specialized it to sword-drawing; Greek specialized it to string-plucking. The shared kinesthetic core (sharp pulling motion) is a genuine semantic connection. One step: sharp pull/draw -> pluck a string.
key_insight: Arabic sharp drawing/unsheathing and Greek string-plucking share the kinesthetic core of sharp pulling motion.
